document_text:
Speech Codec Probing from Semantic and Phonetic Perspectives
Xuan ShiID1,∗,∗∗, Chang Zeng∗, Tiantian Feng1,∗, Shih-Heng Wang1, Jianbo Ma2, Shrikanth
Narayanan1
1University of Southern California, USA
2Dolby Laboratories, USA
xuanshi@usc.edu
Abstract
Speech tokenizers are essential for connecting speech to large
language models (LLMs) in multimodal systems. These to-
kenizers are expected to preserve both semantic and acous-
tic information for downstream understanding and generation.
However, emerging evidence suggests that what is termed ”se-
mantic” in speech representations does not align with text-
derived semantics—a mismatch that can degrade multimodal
LLM performance. In this paper, we systematically analyze
the information encoded by several widely used speech tokeniz-
ers, disentangling their semantic and phonetic content through
word-level probing tasks, layerwise representation analysis, and
cross-modal alignment metrics such as CKA. Our results show
that current tokenizers primarily capture phonetic rather than
lexical-semantic structure, and we derive practical implications
for the design of next-generation speech tokenization methods.
Index Terms: speech codec, semantic, phonetic, attributes
probing
1. Introduction
The rapid advancement of large language models (LLMs) has
driven significant interest in extending their sequence modeling
capabilities to the speech modality. A growing body of research,
including GPT-4o [1], Qwen2.5-Omni [2], and Moshi [3],
demonstrates the potential of unified multimodal large lan-
guage models (MLLMs) that can simultaneously understand
and generate both text and speech. Central to these systems is
the speech tokenizer, which converts continuous speech wave-
forms into discrete token sequences amenable to the autoregres-
sive modeling paradigm of LLMs, enabling a shared vocabu-
lary and modeling framework for diverse tasks spanning auto-
matic speech recognition (ASR), spoken language understand-
ing, text-to-speech synthesis, and voice conversion [4, 5, 6].
The design of speech tokenizers has gradually evolved from
pure signal compression to semantic-aware encoding. Early
neural audio codecs such as SoundStream [7], EnCodec [8], and
DAC [9] employ convolutional encoder-decoder architectures
with Residual Vector Quantization (RVQ) [7], optimizing re-
construction fidelity through perceptual and adversarial losses.
While effective at preserving acoustic details, their represen-
tations are not explicitly designed to capture linguistic content
[7, 8, 9]. To address this, AudioLM [10] introduced a two-stage
paradigm that separates “semantic tokens” derived from SSL
models (e.g., HuBERT [11]) from “acoustic tokens” produced
by neural codecs. This semantic-acoustic decomposition has
since become widely adopted [12], with MIMI [3] internalizing
*These authors contributed equally.
**indicates the corresponding author.it by distilling WavLM [13] features into its first codebook layer.
More recently, end-to-end systems such as Qwen2.5-Omni [2]
and MiMo-Audio [4] directly jointly optimize speech tokenizer
and LLM.
However, a critical question remains: does the informa-
tion labeled as “semantic” in speech tokens genuinely reflect
linguistic meaning? Choi et al. [14] demonstrated that self-
supervised learning (SSL) speech representations are more pho-
netic than semantic, exhibiting greater similarity between near-
homophones (e.g., “accept” and “except”) than between syn-
onyms (e.g., “big” and “large”). This suggests that the “seman-
tic tokens”may more accurately be characterized as phonetic
representations. If this is the case, the misalignment between
phonetic speech tokens and semantic text tokens may contribute
to the performance degradation observed in MLLMs on speech
understanding tasks [15, 16]. Therefore, probing the informa-
tion encoded at each codebook layer is essential for understand-
ing the algorithmic limitations and guiding future design.
In this paper, we systematically probe the knowledge en-
coded in speech codec representations from both semantic and
phonetic perspectives. We first clarify the conceptual distinc-
tion between “semantic” and “phonetic” as used throughout our
analysis in Sec. 2.1. We then conduct three complementary
probing experiments across four representative speech codecs,
namely EnCodec [8], DAC [9], MIMI [3], and MIMO [4], that
span diverse architectural designs and training paradigms: (1)
we first extend the SSL-based probing analysis [14] to neural
codecs to examine the semantic-phonetic knowledge encoded
in their representation. Specifically, we conduct this probing
analysis using synonym and near-homophone word pairs in Sec.
2.3; (2) We then introduce an articulatory phonetic probing
analysis using V ocal Tract Distance (VTD) features [17, 18] ex-
tracted from real-time Magnetic Resonance Imaging (rt-MRI).
This experiment provides a physiologically grounded assess-
ment of phonetic encoding in Sec. 2.4; and (3) we finally eval-
uate the cross-modal semantic alignment between speech and
text token spaces using Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA) in
Sec. 2.5. Through these analyses, we aim to provide actionable
insights regarding the nature of information captured by current
speech tokenizers, and to inform the design of future codecs that
better serve language model integration.
2. Probing Experiment Settings
In this section, we introduce the experiment settings for the
probing experiments regarding semantic and phonetic distribu-
tion in speech codecs.arXiv:2603.10371v1  [eess.AS]  11 Mar 2026

2.1. Probing Task: Semantic and Phonetic
Within the speech processing community, the term “seman-
tic” is often used to describe representations derived from
self-supervised speech models such as HuBERT [11] and
WavLM [13]. However, this usage conflates two fundamentally
distinct linguistic concepts.
In this paper, we adopt the following definitions.Semantic
refers to lexical meaning — the level at which synonyms such
as “big” and “large” are considered close.Phoneticrefers to
speech production — the level at which near-homophones such
as “accept” and “except” are considered similar.
2.2. Model Selection
We select four representative speech codec models for our prob-
ing experiments: EnCodec [8], DAC [9], MIMI [3], and MIMO
[4]. These models span a diverse range of architectural designs,
training strategies, and intended applications, enabling a com-
prehensive analysis of how different codec design choices affect
the amount of semantic and phonetic information preserved in
the learned discrete units. Table 1 summarizes the key design
choices of each model.
EnCodec[8] employs a convolutional encoder-decoder ar-
chitecture (SEANet [19]) with Residual Vector Quantization
(RVQ) [20, 21]. It is trained end-to-end with a combination
of reconstruction, perceptual, and adversarial losses. As one of
the earliest and most widely adopted neural audio codecs, En-
Codec is a pure compression model without explicit semantic
or phonetic modeling.
DAC[9] builds upon the RVQ-based codec framework with
several architectural improvements, including factorized and
L2-normalized codebook vectors [22] for better codebook uti-
lization, snake activation functions [23] for improved periodic
signal modeling, and quantizer dropout during training.
MIMI[3] is the codec component of the Moshi real-time di-
alogue system. Its key distinguishing feature is a “semantic”-
acoustic disentangled codebook design: the first quantizer layer
is distilled from WavLM [13], a SSL model, to capture higher-
level “semantic” representations, while the remaining layers en-
code residual acoustic information through RVQ.
MIMO-Audio-Tokenizer[4] (hereafter referred to as
“MIMO”) is a Transformer-based codec developed within the
MiMo-Audio framework. Unlike the codecs above, MIMO is
trained from scratch through a two-stage process: joint audio
reconstruction and ASR alignment with a LLM, followed by
adversarial fine-tuning.
2.3. Probing Semantic and Phonetic Knowledge in Codecs
In this section, we investigate how much semantic and pho-
netic knowledge is preserved in different speech codebooks. We
adopt the experimental design from a prior probing study on
SSL speech models [14], motivated by the structural similarities
between SSL models and speech codecs. Following [14], se-
mantic and phonetic information density can be estimated via a
proxy task that measures the feature distance between synonym
and near-homophone word pairs. Specifically, a larger feature
distance between synonym pairs relative to near-homophone
pairs indicates a relative higher concentration of phonetic in-
formation, and vice versa.
Data ConstructionWe follow the methodology of [14] to con-
struct synonym and near-homophone word pairs. Individual
word segments are extracted from the LibriSpeech [24] dataset
using alignment timestamps generated by the Montreal Forced
(a)Articulator Boundary
 (b)Vocal Tract Distance
Figure 1:Visualization of vocal tract mid-sagittal rt-MRI
frames and corresponding VTD annotations.
Aligner (MFA) [25]. For each word, synonyms are retrieved
from the cognitive synonym sets in WordNet [26], a large-
scale English lexical database. Regarding near-homophones,
all words are first converted into phonemes using the CMU
Pronouncing Dictionary [27]. We then calculate the phonetic
similarity between words using the Levenshtein distance [28];
word pairs with a normalized Levenshtein distance of less than
0.4 are classified as near-homophone pairs [14]. While this
study primarily utilizes English datasets due to the availability
of well-characterized resources, we note that prior work in [14]
has demonstrated consistent trends in cross-lingual scenarios.
Feature ExtractionWe extract the accumulated decoded
speech features from each codec layer. Specifically, the fea-
ture at a given layer is defined as the summation of the decoded
output from the current quantizer layer and the accumulated fea-
tures from all preceding layers. Since many speech codecs use
Euclidean Residual Vector Quantizer, we employ the Euclidean
distance as a standardized metric to compute the distances be-
tween these high-dimensional feature vectors. For more techni-
cal implementation details, we refer the reader to [14].
2.4. Articulatory Phonetic Probing
To further quantitatively investigate the phonetic information
encoded within speech codecs, we examine the correlation be-
tween articulatory dynamics and speech codec features. Specif-
ically, we extract an articulatory feature, namely V ocal Tract
Distance (VTD) [17, 18], from the mid-sagittal plane of rt-MRI
sequences. As illustrated in Fig. 1, VTD represents the length
of the intersections between the articulator boundaries and a set
of gridlines [17]. These gridlines are evenly distributed from
the lips to the larynx and are oriented perpendicular to the vocal
tract midline. VTD effectively captures the shape of the airway
tunnel during speech, providing a straightforward and compre-
hensive representation of phonetic information.
DatasetOur articulatory analysis is conducted on the 75-
Speaker dataset [29] and the 75-Speaker Annot-16 subset [30]
(hereafter referred to as “Annot-16”). The 75-Speaker dataset
is a multimodal corpus containing simultaneous speech and
rt-MRI video. This American English corpus comprises var-
ious speech materials, including vowel-consonant sequences,
scripted reading, and spontaneous speech. Annot-16 provides
expert-labeled articulator boundary annotations for 16 speakers
from the original dataset.
Evaluation MetricsWe employ Projection Weighted Canon-
ical Correlation Analysis (PWCCA) [31] to measure the cor-
relation between VTD and speech codec features. The VTD
features are sampled at 83 Hz, matching the rt-MRI frame rate.
For each speaker, the number of gridlines is calibrated to 120,

Table 1:Comparison of different speech tokenizers across various dimensions. SR denotes sampling rate. Input and Output SR show
the configuration we used in probing experiments.
Speech Tokenizer Input SR (Hz) Output SR (bps) Training Objectives Application Tasks
EnCodec [8] 24k 12k Reconstruction Audio Compression
DAC [9] 24k 24k Reconstruction Audio Compression
MIMI [3] 24k 4.4k Reconstruction and Distillation Conversational AI
MIMO [4] 24k 1.55k Reconstruction and ASR Conversational AI
where each index corresponds to a specific articulatory loca-
tion. Consequently, the VTD is a time-sequence feature with
a dimensionality of 120. To address the discrepancy in sam-
pling rates and dimensions between VTD and codec features,
we up-sample the VTD sequences to align with the codec fea-
tures, ensuring a robust PWCCA calculation.
2.5. Semantic Alignment between Text and Speech
One of the primary applications of speech codecs is the conver-
sion of raw audio into discrete speech tokens for use in speech
language models (SLMs). Inspired by [32], we assume that the
latent representation from text and speech, even though they are
derived from different modalities, tend to move to a common
representation space during joint training. Motivated by this ob-
servation, we extract the latent representations that are decoded
from text and speech tokens before LLM processing and mea-
sure the similarities between corresponding text-speech pairs in
the shared representation space. In this experiment, we choose
MIMI [3] and MIMO [4] models for measuring representation
similarities as they are originally designed for conversational AI
and their compatible LLMs are accessible.
DatasetWe follow the word-level speech extraction pipeline
described in Sec. 2.3 to extract speech segments from the Lib-
riSpeech dataset [24].
Evaluation MetricsWhile alignment between text and speech
is desirable, the latent feature spaces for different modalities
in multimodal large language models (MLLMs) are not nec-
essarily shared, which poses a significant challenge for direct
evaluation. Therefore, we adopt the Centered Kernel Align-
ment (CKA) [33] to quantify the structural similarity between
the speech and text modalities.
3. Probing Experiment Findings
3.1. Semantic and Phonetic Analysis
We use the Euclidean distance between speech features ex-
tracted from word pairs as a proxy to evaluate how different
codecs capture target semantic and phonetic information. As
illustrated in Fig. 2, the top and bottom rows present the abso-
lute Euclidean distances and the normalized feature distances,
respectively. The normalized feature distance is calculated by
subtracting the distances of the random setting.
From the absolute distance plots, we observe that as the
codebook depth increases, EnCodec fluctuates without a clear
pattern across all word-pair settings. In contrast, the remaining
speech codecs exhibit a consistent increase in Euclidean dis-
tance as the codebook layer increases, indicating a progressive
accumulation of information.
The normalized distributions in the bottom row provide a
clearer perspective on the relative information density of seman-
tic versus phonetic attributes.
As the codebooks go deeper, most curves approach the ran-dom line. This trend suggests a ”fading” effect of distinguish-
able semantic and phonetic information within the speech to-
kens. From EnCodec and DAC, we observe the fading in a
drastic and gradual pattern, respectively. This fading is particu-
larly evident for semantic information: the synonym curves for
both EnCodec and DAC frequently overlap with or even exceed
the random baseline starting from the early-to-mid codebook
stages. Notably, DAC maintains significantly higher distances
for speaker attributes compared to synonyms and homophones,
underscoring its superior modeling capability in capturing tim-
bre and fine-grained acoustic details. We attribute the fading
effect in EnCodec and DAC, especially regarding semantic in-
formation, to the training objectives of these codec models, as
they did not incorporate semantic-related tasks during training.
Both MIMI and MIMO demonstrate similar architectural
trends: semantic, phonetic, and speaker-related information
accumulate through the codebook layers. However, MIMI
exhibits an earlier convergence of information and contains
a higher proportion of phonetic (homophone) and acoustic
(speaker) information. This behavior is likely attributed to
MIMI’s first codebook being distilled from WavLM, which in-
jects robust phonetic priors.
Overall, the evaluation across all four speech codecs consis-
tently reveals that they preserve a substantially higher propor-
tion of phonetic information relative to semantic information.
The above analysis establishes, from a functional perspec-
tive, that speech codecs consistently encode more phonetic than
semantic information. However, this finding is derived from an
indirect proxy task based on word-pair distances in the feature
space. A natural question arises: does this phonetic dominance
reflect a genuine encoding of speech production mechanisms,
or is it merely an artifact of acoustic similarity? To answer this,
we turn to articulatory evidence in the following section.
3.2. Articulation Phonetic Analysis
To provide physiological grounding for the phonetic dominance
observed in Sec. 4.1, we examine the correlation between codec
features and articulatory dynamics measured by VTD, as shown
in Fig. 3. Cross validated with Fig 2, EnCodec and DAC demon-
strate the fading of phonetic information as the correlation curve
keep go down in Fig. 3. Likewise, MIMI and MIMO show the
phonetic accumulation with the codebooks go deeper. The early
converge point also appear in the phonetic correlation from
MIMI and MIMO.
Phonetic Analysis for MIMIIn the MIMI architecture [3], the
feature from the first codebook layer, commonly referred to as
the “semantic” feature, is distilled from WavLM [13]. However,
since WavLM is primarily trained on ASR and acoustic-related
tasks, its representations may not directly align with the linguis-
tic “semantic” concept introduced in Sec. 2.1. To investigate
this, we separately calculated the phonetic correlation between
the first-layer feature and the accumulated features of the re-
maining layers using VTD. A comparison between Fig. 4 and

(a)EnCodec
 (b)DAC
 (c)MIMI
 (d)MIMO
(e)EnCodec - Norm
 (f)DAC - Norm
 (g)MIMI - Norm
 (h)MIMO - Norm
Figure 2:Phonetic and Semantic Information Distribution
(a)EnCodec
 (b)DAC
 (c)MIMI
 (d)MIMO
Figure 3:Articulation Phonetic Correlation
Fig. 3c clearly reveals that distillation from WavLM injects sig-
nificant phonetic-related knowledge. Furthermore, even with-
out the first layer, as the acoustic codebook layers go deeper, the
speech features from these layers also accumulate a substantial
portion of phonetic information.
Figure 4:MIMI - Separate Phonetic Analysis
3.3. Semantic Alignment
The preceding analyses consistently show that codec tokens
lack semantic information. A direct consequence would be
weak structural alignment between speech codec token and
text token. To test this, we measure Centered Kernel Align-
ment (CKA) between the two spaces. Both MIMI and MIMO
yield CKA scores well below 1.0 (0.329 and 0.122, respec-
tively), confirming a substantial cross-modal structural gap. To
control for spurious alignment caused by each space’s intrinsic
geometry, we also compute a random-permutation baseline that
breaks the speech–text pairing. Both codecs show only mod-
est gains over this baseline (∆=0.087for MIMI;∆=0.054for
MIMO), indicating that neither codec captures strong semantic
structure—consistent with Fig. 2. MIMI’s higher absolute CKA
is likely an artifact of the low effective dimensionality inher-
ited from its WavLM-distilled first layer, which inflates chance
alignment; the baseline-corrected∆provides a fairer compari-son.
3.4. Discussion
Across three complementary analyses, our results paint a con-
sistent picture: speech codecs preserve substantially more pho-
netic than semantic information, this phonetic encoding is
grounded in articulatory production mechanisms, and the re-
sulting representations show weak structural alignment with
text semantics. These findings suggest two potential directions
for improving semantic encoding in speech tokenizers. First,
since distillation from SSL speech models inherits their pho-
netic bias (as demonstrated in Sec. 3.2), future tokenizers may
benefit from distilling representations from models with gen-
uine text semantic understanding, such as the text embeddings
of LLMs or cross-modal encoders trained on paired speech-text
data. Second, the training objective itself could be augmented
with explicit semantic constraints, for instance by encourag-
ing codec representations of synonymous words to be closer in
the latent space, thereby complementing acoustic reconstruction
with semantic structure preservation.
4. Conclusion
This paper systematically probes the information encoded in
speech codec representations from semantic and phonetic per-
spectives. Through functional analysis, articulatory probing
with rt-MRI data, and cross-modal alignment evaluation, ap-
plied across four representative codec frameworks, we demon-
strate that current speech codecs consistently encode more pho-
netic than semantic information. The articulatory evidence con-
firms that this phonetic dominance reflects genuine speech pro-
duction mechanisms. Our analysis of MIMI further reveals that

distillation from WavLM injects phonetic rather than semantic
knowledge, challenging the widespread use of the term “seman-
tic tokens.” These findings highlight the need for future work
on tokenizer designs that incorporate explicit semantic objec-
tives to better serve language model integration.
5. Generative AI Use Disclosure
We used Gemini 3.1 and Claude Opus 4.6 for paper proofread-
ing. All substantive contents remain the authors’ own.

structure.title: Introduction
structure.summary: The partial document discusses speech tokenizers and their role in connecting speech to large language models in multimodal systems. It highlights the importance of preserving both semantic and acoustic information in speech tokenizers for downstream understanding and generation. The document analyzes the information encoded by several widely used speech tokenizers, disentangling their semantic and phonetic content through word-level probing tasks, layerwise representation analysis, and cross-modal alignment metrics. The main points covered include the limitations of current speech tokenizers, the distinction between semantic and phonetic information, and the need for a more nuanced understanding of the information captured by speech tokenizers to inform the design of future codecs that better serve language model integration. The document also introduces a probing experiment setting to investigate the semantic and phonetic knowledge preserved in different speech codebooks, using methods such as synonym and near-homophone word pairs, articulatory phonetic probing, and cross-modal semantic alignment.

structure.title: Probing Experiment Settings
structure.summary: The partial document discusses speech processing, specifically probing tasks for semantic and phonetic knowledge in speech codecs. It defines semantic as referring to lexical meaning and phonetic as referring to speech production. The document selects four speech codec models for analysis, including EnCodec, DAC, MIMI, and MIMO, which vary in architectural design, training strategies, and applications. It investigates how much semantic and phonetic knowledge is preserved in different speech codebooks using a proxy task that measures feature distance between synonym and near-homophone word pairs. The document also explores articulatory phonetic probing by examining the correlation between articulatory dynamics and speech codec features, using Vocal Tract Distance (VTD) as an articulatory feature. The analysis is conducted on the 75-Speaker dataset and the Annot-16 subset, using Projection Weighted Canonical Correlation Analysis (PWCCA) as the evaluation metric.

nodes.title: Probing Semantic and Phonetic Knowledge in Codecs
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses speech processing, specifically probing tasks for semantic and phonetic knowledge in speech codecs. It defines semantic as referring to lexical meaning and phonetic as referring to speech production. The document selects four speech codec models for analysis, including EnCodec, DAC, MIMI, and MIMO, which vary in architectural design, training strategies, and applications. It investigates how much semantic and phonetic knowledge is preserved in different speech codebooks through probing experiments, using a proxy task to measure feature distance between synonym and near-homophone word pairs. nodes.title: Articulatory Phonetic Probing
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses speech processing, specifically probing tasks for semantic and phonetic knowledge in speech codecs. It defines semantic and phonetic concepts, selecting four representative speech codec models for analysis. The document investigates how much semantic and phonetic knowledge is preserved in different speech codebooks, adopting an experimental design from a prior probing study on self-supervised speech models. It also explores articulatory phonetic probing, examining the correlation between articulatory dynamics and speech codec features using Vocal Tract Distance (VTD) as an articulatory feature. The analysis is conducted on various datasets, including LibriSpeech and 75-Speaker, using metrics such as feature distance and Projection Weighted Canonical Correlation Analysis (PWCCA) to measure the correlation between VTD and speech codec features.

nodes.title: Semantic Alignment between Text and Speech
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the analysis of speech tokenizers, which are essential for connecting speech to large language models in multimodal systems. The main points covered include the distinction between semantic and phonetic information in speech representations, the probing of several widely used speech tokenizers to disentangle their semantic and phonetic content, and the evaluation of the cross-modal semantic alignment between speech and text token spaces. The document also introduces an articulatory phonetic probing analysis using Vocal Tract Distance features extracted from real-time Magnetic Resonance Imaging. The results show that current tokenizers primarily capture phonetic rather than lexical-semantic structure, and the document derives practical implications for the design of next-generation speech tokenization methods.

structure.title: Probing Experiment Findings
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the comparison of different speech tokenizers, including EnCodec, DAC, MIMI, and MIMO, across various dimensions such as input and output sampling rates, training objectives, and application tasks. The document also explores the semantic alignment between text and speech, using metrics such as Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA) to quantify the structural similarity between speech and text modalities. Additionally, the document presents the findings of probing experiments, including semantic and phonetic analysis, which reveal that speech codecs consistently encode more phonetic than semantic information. The analysis also examines the correlation between codec features and articulatory dynamics, providing physiological grounding for the phonetic dominance observed in the speech codecs. Overall, the document provides insights into the capabilities and limitations of different speech tokenizers and their potential applications in speech language models and conversational AI.

nodes.title: Articulation Phonetic Analysis
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the comparison of different speech tokenizers, including EnCodec, DAC, MIMI, and MIMO, across various dimensions such as input and output sampling rates, training objectives, and application tasks. The document also explores the semantic alignment between text and speech, using metrics like Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA) to quantify the structural similarity between speech and text modalities. Additionally, the document presents the findings of probing experiments, which analyze the capture of semantic and phonetic information by different speech codecs, revealing that they preserve a higher proportion of phonetic information relative to semantic information. The document also examines the correlation between codec features and articulatory dynamics, providing physiological grounding for the phonetic dominance observed in the speech codecs. Overall, the document provides insights into the capabilities and limitations of various speech tokenizers and their applications in conversational AI and speech language models.

nodes.title: Semantic Alignment
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the comparison of different speech tokenizers across various dimensions, including their input and output sampling rates, training objectives, and application tasks. It also explores the semantic alignment between text and speech, using metrics such as Centered Kernel Alignment (CKA) to quantify the structural similarity between speech and text modalities. The document presents the results of probing experiments, which reveal that speech codecs preserve substantially more phonetic than semantic information, and this phonetic encoding is grounded in articulatory production mechanisms. The results also show weak structural alignment between speech codec tokens and text tokens, suggesting potential directions for improving semantic encoding in speech tokenizers, such as distilling representations from models with genuine text semantic understanding or augmenting the training objective with explicit semantic constraints.

nodes.title: Discussion
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the information encoded in speech codec representations, focusing on the phonetic and semantic aspects. It presents various analyses, including functional analysis, articulatory probing, and cross-modal alignment evaluation, to demonstrate that current speech codecs consistently encode more phonetic than semantic information. The document highlights the phonetic dominance in speech codecs, which is grounded in articulatory production mechanisms, and suggests potential directions for improving semantic encoding in speech tokenizers, such as distilling representations from models with genuine text semantic understanding or augmenting the training objective with explicit semantic constraints.

structure.title: Conclusion
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the information encoded in speech codec representations from semantic and phonetic perspectives. It analyzes four representative codec frameworks, including EnCodec, DAC, MIMI, and MIMO, and reveals that current speech codecs consistently encode more phonetic than semantic information. The document also explores the articulatory evidence that confirms the phonetic dominance reflects genuine speech production mechanisms. Furthermore, it examines the distillation from WavLM and its impact on phonetic and semantic knowledge. The findings highlight the need for future work on tokenizer designs that incorporate explicit semantic objectives to better serve language model integration. The document also references various studies and techniques related to speech processing, language modeling, and neural networks. Overall, the main points covered in the partial document include the phonetic and semantic information distribution in speech codecs, the articulatory correlation, and the need for improved semantic encoding in speech tokenizers.

structure.title: Generative AI Use Disclosure
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the integration of language models with speech processing, highlighting the need for tokenizer designs that incorporate explicit semantic objectives. It challenges the widespread use of the term "semantic tokens" and suggests that distillation from WavLM injects phonetic rather than semantic knowledge. The document also references various studies and models related to speech processing, language modeling, and neural audio compression, including Gemini, Claude, GPT-4, and WavLM. Additionally, it mentions the use of generative AI tools for paper proofreading and provides a list of references to relevant research papers and technical reports. Overall, the document focuses on the intersection of natural language processing and speech processing, emphasizing the importance of developing more effective and semantically-aware tokenization methods for language model integration.

structure.title: References
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the integration of language models with speech processing, highlighting the need for tokenizer designs that incorporate explicit semantic objectives. It challenges the widespread use of the term "semantic tokens" and suggests that distillation from WavLM injects phonetic rather than semantic knowledge. The document also references various studies and models related to speech processing, language modeling, and generative AI, including Gemini, Claude, and WavLM. Additionally, it touches on the use of vector quantization techniques and the importance of understanding the modality gap between speech and text. The references provided are extensive, covering a range of topics from speech representation learning to neural audio compression and language modeling. Overall, the document appears to be focused on the development of more effective and integrated speech and language processing systems.
